questions.question_text: What is Tennessee's position on net listings?
questions.correct_answer: Discouraged due to conflicts of interest
questions.explanation: Net listings are discouraged in Tennessee because they create a conflict of interest — the broker is incentivized to get the lowest possible price for the seller to maximize their own profit.

questions.question_text: What must every Tennessee listing agreement include regarding duration?
questions.explanation: Listing agreements must have a definite termination date. Open-ended listings are not permitted because they could trap sellers in indefinite arrangements.

questions.question_text: A broker creates a listing agreement that does not specify the compensation arrangement. What is the issue?
questions.correct_answer: Compensation terms must be clearly stated
distractors: No issue if the standard rate applies
Only an issue for exclusive listings
The MLS will set the compensation
questions.explanation: Listing agreements must clearly state compensation terms. Vague or missing compensation arrangements can lead to disputes and constitute improper listing practices.

questions.question_text: A listing has no expiration date and the seller wants to cancel after 6 months. What protection should the listing have included?
questions.correct_answer: A definite termination date as required by law
questions.explanation: Tennessee requires a definite termination date in listing agreements. Without one, the listing is improper and may be unenforceable. This protects sellers from being locked into indefinite agreements.

questions.question_text: In a net listing, a broker lists a home for $250,000 net to the seller. The home sells for $310,000. Why is this arrangement problematic?
questions.correct_answer: The seller misses out on the $60,000 difference
distractors: The buyer always pays more than market value
TREC must pre-approve all net listing amounts
questions.explanation: In a net listing, the seller receives only the agreed net amount ($250,000) while the broker keeps the entire difference ($60,000). The seller may not realize the property's true market value, and the broker has no incentive to share the upside.

questions.question_text: Which state agency administers Tennessee's fair housing laws?
questions.explanation: The Tennessee Human Rights Commission (THRC) administers the Tennessee Human Rights Act, which provides state fair housing protections. TREC handles licensing, not fair housing enforcement.

questions.question_text: Beyond the seven federal protected classes, Tennessee's Human Rights Act also protects which additional class?
questions.explanation: Tennessee's Human Rights Act adds 'creed' as a protected class beyond the seven federal categories (race, color, religion, national origin, sex, disability, familial status).

questions.question_text: An agent steers minority buyers away from certain neighborhoods. Where can a complaint be filed?
distractors: Only with the local police department
questions.explanation: Fair housing complaints can be filed with the Tennessee Human Rights Commission (THRC) or with HUD at the federal level. Additionally, TREC can take disciplinary action against the licensee.

questions.question_text: A licensee violates the Tennessee Human Rights Act. What can TREC do in addition to any THRC action?
distractors: Nothing, fair housing is outside TREC authority
Only issue a written warning letter
questions.explanation: Violation of fair housing laws is grounds for TREC disciplinary action, including license revocation. This is in addition to any enforcement by THRC or HUD.

questions.question_text: Which discriminatory practice involves telling homeowners that minority families are moving in to induce panic selling?
questions.explanation: Blockbusting is the illegal practice of inducing panic selling by suggesting that protected class members are moving into an area, leading to declining property values.

questions.question_text: Under Tennessee's SB 909 (2025), what must a real estate wholesaler disclose to the seller?
questions.correct_answer: Their intent to market or assign the contract
distractors: The identity of the end buyer only
The expected profit from the assignment
questions.explanation: SB 909 requires wholesalers to disclose to the seller, before signing, their intent to market or assign their interest in the contract. This must be in writing in bold/large print.

questions.question_text: A wholesaler assigns a purchase contract to an end buyer. How much advance notice must the wholesaler give the seller?
questions.correct_answer: At least 3 business days before the assignment
distractors: At least 10 business days before closing
At least 24 hours before the assignment
questions.explanation: Under SB 909, if a wholesaler assigns the contract, they must notify the seller of the effective date of the assignment at least 3 business days in advance.

questions.question_text: How must wholesaling disclosures be presented under Tennessee's SB 909?
questions.correct_answer: In writing, bold/large print, in the contract
distractors: Verbally at the time of contract signing
In a separate email after the contract
In any format the wholesaler chooses
questions.explanation: SB 909 requires all disclosures to be in writing, in bold/large-font print, and included in the purchase contract or an addendum. Verbal disclosure does not satisfy the requirement.

questions.question_text: What is the statute of limitations for claims under Tennessee's wholesaling disclosure law?
questions.correct_answer: Two years from the purchase contract date
distractors: One year from the closing date
Three years from the assignment date
Five years from the date of discovery
questions.explanation: SB 909 sets a two-year statute of limitations for claims, measured from the date of the original purchase contract.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is considered prohibited misconduct for Tennessee licensees?
questions.correct_answer: Inducing a party to breach an existing contract
distractors: Declining to take an overpriced listing
Recommending a home inspector to a buyer
Suggesting the seller get a second opinion
questions.explanation: Inducing breach of contract is specifically prohibited. Licensees must not encourage parties to break existing contractual obligations in order to create a new transaction.
